description: Standard 'not medically necessary' denial for residential treatment

conversation.content: We just got a letter from Cigna denying my daughter's residential treatment. It says 'the requested service is not medically necessary based on the clinical information provided.' She's been struggling with opioid addiction for 3 years. What does this mean and what can we do?

must_explain: not medically necessary is the most common denial reason
appeal deadline exists and should be checked in the letter

description: Concurrent denial — coverage cut while patient is in treatment

conversation.content: The insurance company just called the facility and said they won't authorize any more days. My son has been in inpatient for 8 days and the clinical team says he needs at least 21 more days. They're saying he might get discharged this weekend. I'm panicking.

must_explain: this is a concurrent denial / utilization review denial
facility should file a concurrent review appeal
patient cannot be forcibly discharged solely due to insurance denial
must_not_say: you have to take him home

description: Potential parity violation — behavioral health treated differently than medical equivalent

conversation.content: My insurance approved 30 days of inpatient rehab for my husband's heart surgery recovery without any questions. But for my son's substance use residential treatment, they denied it after 5 days saying 'outpatient is sufficient.' The clinical team disagrees. A friend told me there's some kind of law about this being unfair. Is that true?

must_explain: Mental Health Parity and Addiction Equity Act (MHPAEA)
insurance must apply same criteria to behavioral health as medical/surgical
this scenario may be a parity violation
can file a parity complaint with state insurance commissioner
must_not_say: insurance companies can do whatever they want
